title: Effect of Pb Substitution at the Mo site on the Magnetic Properties of the Polar Magnet Fe$_2$Mo$_3$O$_8$
authors: Takumi Shirasaki, Taichi Ishikawa, Shungo Nakayama, Hideki Kuwahara
abstract: The ternary transition-metal oxide Fe$_2$Mo$_3$O$_8$ is a polar magnet characterized by a layered structure of magnetic Fe honeycomb lattices and non-magnetic Mo kagome lattices. Whereas previous studies have primarily focused on the chemical substitution at the Fe sites to modulate the magnetic properties, the Mo sites have remained largely unexplored due to the strong spin-singlet trimerization of Mo$^{4+}$ ions. In this study, we investigated the effect of substituting non-magnetic Pb$^{4+}$ and Zr$^{4+}$ ions into the Mo sites to intentionally disrupt the Mo trimers. Our results reveal that the disruption of the Mo spin-singlet state induces active spins within the Mo layer, resulting in the emergence of a ferromagnetic-like behavior that persists even at room temperature. Quantitative analysis that takes into account the weight fraction of the main phase suggests an effective spin $S = 1/2$ state per active Mo ion upon trimer disruption. These findings demonstrate that controlling non-magnetic cluster states within a polar host via chemical substitution is a promising approach for designing room-temperature magnetoelectric materials.
summary: This study demonstrates that substituting non-magnetic ions into the Mo sites of the polar magnet Fe2Mo3O8 breaks up spin-singlet clusters, creating active spins that induce room-temperature ferromagnetism. This approach provides a strategy for designing new magnetoelectric materials by controlling non-magnetic cluster states.
main_problem: Investigating how substituting non-magnetic Pb4+ and Zr4+ ions into the Mo sites of the polar magnet Fe2Mo3O8 disrupts Mo spin-singlet trimers to induce new magnetic functionalities.
main_result: The disruption of Mo spin-singlet trimers induces active spins in the Mo layer, leading to the emergence of ferromagnetic-like behavior that persists up to room temperature.
method: Solid-state reaction synthesis followed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Rietveld refinement and magnetic measurements using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) up to 8 T.
model_or_system: The system is the ternary transition-metal oxide Fe2Mo3O8, a polar magnet with a layered structure of magnetic Fe honeycomb lattices and non-magnetic Mo kagome lattices containing Mo4+ spin-singlet trimers.
key_observables: Magnetization (M) vs. temperature (T) and magnetic field (H), Neel temperature (TN), and magnetization increment (delta M).
important_parameters: Substitution level (x) from 0 to 0.20, Neel temperature TN approx 60 K, and effective spin S = 1/2 per active Mo ion.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a single dopant ion severs two of the three spin-singlet bonds in a trimer, leaving one intact dimer with localized spins.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material and the problem of Mo-site substitution, describes the experimental synthesis and structural characterization, presents magnetic measurement results showing emergent ferromagnetism, and provides a quantitative statistical model to explain the observed magnetization increase.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a) Crystal structure of the M2Mo3O8 system (space group P63mc) visualized using VESTA [2]. The magnetic M2+ layers and Mo4+ layers are alternately stacked along the c-axis. (b) Honeycomb lattice formed by M2+ ions, occupying inequivalent octahedral and tetrahedral sites. (c) Kagome-like lattice formed by Mo4+ ions, in which three adjacent Mo ions form a spin- singlet trimer, rendering the layer non-magnetic.

Fig. 2. (a) Powder X-ray diffraction patterns of polycrystalline Fe2(Mo1−xPbx)3O8 at room temperature (RT). Black and white triangles indicate peaks from MoO2 and PbMoO4, respectively. Asterisks denote peaks from unidentified impurities. (b) Powder X-ray diffraction patterns of polycrystalline Fe2(Mo1−xZrx)3O8 at RT. Black triangles indicate MoO2 peaks, and asterisks denote unidentified impurity peaks.

Fig. 4. Magnetic field dependence of the magnetization for polycrystalline Fe2(Mo1−xPbx)3O8 (x = 0, 0.05, 0.10) at 5 K (open symbols) and 300 K (solid symbols). The inset shows the change in magnetization for x = 0.05 relative to the pristine x = 0 sample, ∆M = M(x = 0.05) −M(x = 0) (see text for details).

Fig. 3. Temperature dependence of the magnetization for polycrystalline Fe2(Mo1−xPbx)3O8 (x = 0, 0.05, 0.10). The dashed line indicates the antiferromagnetic transition temperature (TN), and black triangles mark the magnetic anomalies observed for x = 0.05 and 0.10. The inset illustrates a schematic spin configuration model that effectively explains the experimental results when a single Pb ion substitutes into a Mo trimer. The up and down arrows enclosed in a circle indicate a spin-singlet pair (see text for details).

Fig. 5. Magnetic field dependence of the magnetization for polycrystalline Fe2(Mo1−xZrx)3O8 (x = 0, 0.05, 0.10) at 300 K. The inset shows the temperature dependence of the magnetization measured under zero-field- cooled (ZFC) conditions. The dashed line and black triangles indicate TN and the magnetic anomalies, respectively.
